examples.explanation: Array: [10,15,20,25], n=4. Initialize count=0. Check pairs: (10,15): gcd(10,15)=5>1, count=1. (10,20): gcd(10,20)=10>1, count=2. (10,25): gcd(10,25)=5>1, count=3. (15,20): gcd(15,20)=5>1, count=4. (15,25): gcd(15,25)=5>1, count=5. (20,25): gcd(20,25)=5>1, count=6. But wait, we only consider i<j, so pairs are: (10,15), (10,20), (10,25), (15,20), (15,25), (20,25). All have gcd>1, so count=6? Let's recalc: Actually, for i=0,j=1: gcd(10,15)=5>1, count=1. i=0,j=2: gcd(10,20)=10>1, count=2. i=0,j=3: gcd(10,25)=5>1, count=3. i=1,j=2: gcd(15,20)=5>1, count=4. i=1,j=3: gcd(15,25)=5>1, count=5. i=2,j=3: gcd(20,25)=5>1, count=6. So output should be 6, but I said 3 earlier—correction: output is 6. Let's verify: All pairs have common factors, so yes, 6 pairs. I'll correct in test cases.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n² * log(max))
approaches.summary: Iterate over all pairs of numbers in the array and compute their GCD directly using Euclidean algorithm, counting those with GCD > 1. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Pairs with GCD > 1 Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of counting all pairs (i, j) where i < j and gcd(arr[i], arr[j]) > 1 using nested loops and Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to count pairs with GCD greater than 1. The intuition is straightforward: we need to examine every possible pair in the array and check if their greatest common divisor exceeds 1. Since we're counting pairs where i < j, we avoid counting duplicates like (j, i). The Euclidean algorithm efficiently computes GCD in O(log(min(a,b))) time, making the pair checking efficient despite the O(n²) pairs. The algorithm works by initializing a count to 0, then using two nested loops: the outer loop iterates i from 0 to n-2, and the inner loop iterates j from i+1 to n-1. For each pair (arr[i], arr[j]), we compute gcd(arr[i], arr[j]) using the Euclidean algorithm. If the result is greater than 1, we increment the count. After checking all pairs, we return the count. The time complexity is O(n² * log(max)), where n is the array length and max is the maximum value in the array, due to n² pairs and log(max) for GCD computation. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach is inefficient for large n but serves as a baseline. Common mistakes include counting pairs where i ≥ j, which would double-count or include self-pairs, and not handling large numbers efficiently in GCD computation. The connection to L1 is that this level uses the Euclidean GCD function as a key subroutine.

examples.trace: Array: [10,15,20,25], n=4. Initialize count=0. Check pairs: (10,15): gcd(10,15)=5>1, count=1. (10,20): gcd(10,20)=10>1, count=2. (10,25): gcd(10,25)=5>1, count=3. (15,20): gcd(15,20)=5>1, count=4. (15,25): gcd(15,25)=5>1, count=5. (20,25): gcd(20,25)=5>1, count=6. But wait, we only consider i<j, so pairs are: (10,15), (10,20), (10,25), (15,20), (15,25), (20,25). All have gcd>1, so count=6? Let's recalc: Actually, for i=0,j=1: gcd(10,15)=5>1, count=1. i=0,j=2: gcd(10,20)=10>1, count=2. i=0,j=3: gcd(10,25)=5>1, count=3. i=1,j=2: gcd(15,20)=5>1, count=4. i=1,j=3: gcd(15,25)=5>1, count=5. i=2,j=3: gcd(20,25)=5>1, count=6. So output should be 6, but I said 3 earlier—correction: output is 6. Let's verify: All pairs have common factors, so yes, 6 pairs. I'll correct in test cases.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Euclidean Algorithm for GCD Computation
ladders.desc: Implement the Euclidean algorithm to compute the greatest common divisor of two integers efficiently.
ladders.explanation: The Euclidean algorithm is a key subroutine used to compute the GCD of two numbers. The intuition is based on the property that gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b), which reduces the problem size iteratively until b becomes 0, at which point a is the GCD. This works because any common divisor of a and b also divides a mod b, and vice versa. The algorithm steps are: 1) Take two integers a and b. 2) While b is not 0: set a, b = b, a % b. 3) Return the absolute value of a (since GCD is positive). In pseudocode: function gcd(a, b): while b != 0: a, b = b, a % b; return abs(a). Time complexity is O(log(min(a,b))) as the numbers reduce quickly by modulo operation. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the GCD check for each pair. Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers (use absolute values), infinite loops if not updating correctly, and not considering a or b as zero (gcd(0,x)=|x|). Real code pattern: def gcd(a, b): a, b = abs(a), abs(b); while b: a, b = b, a % b; return a.

examples.trace: Start: a=48, b=18. Iteration 1: b=18≠0, so a,b = 18, 48%18=12. Now a=18, b=12. Iteration 2: b=12≠0, so a,b = 12, 18%12=6. Now a=12, b=6. Iteration 3: b=6≠0, so a,b = 6, 12%6=0. Now a=6, b=0. Loop ends since b=0. Return a=6.

Start: a=17, b=13. Iteration 1: b=13≠0, so a,b = 13, 17%13=4. Now a=13, b=4. Iteration 2: b=4≠0, so a,b = 4, 13%4=1. Now a=4, b=1. Iteration 3: b=1≠0, so a,b = 1, 4%1=0. Now a=1, b=0. Loop ends. Return a=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Over All Unique Pairs in an Array
ladders.desc: Generate all unique index pairs (i, j) where i < j in an array without duplication.
ladders.explanation: The core logic for the brute force approach is to iterate over all unique pairs of indices in the array. The intuition is that to count pairs with a certain property, we need to examine each possible pair exactly once. Since pairs (i, j) and (j, i) are considered the same for unordered pairs, we only consider i < j to avoid duplicates. This is done using nested loops: the outer loop variable i runs from 0 to n-2, and the inner loop variable j runs from i+1 to n-1. This ensures that for each i, j is always greater than i, covering all combinations without repetition. The total number of pairs is n*(n-1)/2, which is the number of ways to choose 2 elements from n. In pseudocode: for i in range(0, n-1): for j in range(i+1, n): process pair (arr[i], arr[j]). Time complexity is O(n²) for the pair generation, and space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the pair iteration framework where GCD is computed for each pair. Common mistakes include using j from 0 to n-1, which counts each pair twice, or including i=j, which counts self-pairs. Real code pattern: n = len(arr); for i in range(n): for j in range(i+1, n): # check arr[i] and arr[j].

examples.trace: n=3. Outer loop i from 0 to 1 (since n-2=1). i=0: inner loop j from i+1=1 to 2. j=1: pair (0,1). j=2: pair (0,2). i=1: inner loop j from 2 to 2. j=2: pair (1,2). Total pairs: (0,1), (0,2), (1,2).
n=4. i from 0 to 2. i=0: j from 1 to 3: pairs (0,1),(0,2),(0,3). i=1: j from 2 to 3: pairs (1,2),(1,3). i=2: j from 3 to 3: pair (2,3). Total 6 pairs.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation and Integer Division
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the modulo operation (%) to compute remainders, essential for the Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation is a building block for the Euclidean algorithm. The intuition is that a % b gives the remainder when a is divided by b, which is crucial for reducing numbers in GCD computation. Formally, for integers a and b (b ≠ 0), a % b = a - b * (a // b), where // is integer division. This operation helps in iteratively replacing larger numbers with smaller remainders. For example, in gcd(a,b), we use a, b = b, a % b to shift to smaller numbers. Key properties: 0 ≤ a % b < |b|, and if a % b = 0, then b divides a. In pseudocode, modulo is directly used as the % operator. Time complexity is O(1) per operation, but in GCD, it's used repeatedly. Space is O(1). This feeds into L1 by providing the reduction step in the Euclidean algorithm. Common mistakes include confusion with negative numbers (Python's % always returns a non-negative result), division by zero, and misunderstanding integer division vs float division. Real code pattern: remainder = a % b; used in a loop for GCD.

examples.trace: Compute 17 % 5. 17 divided by 5 is 3 with remainder 2, since 17 = 5*3 + 2. So 17 % 5 = 2.
Compute 10 % 3. 10 divided by 3 is 3 with remainder 1, since 10 = 3*3 + 1. So 10 % 3 = 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Looping Through Array Indices
ladders.desc: Use for-loops to iterate over indices of an array, accessing elements sequentially.
ladders.explanation: Looping through array indices is a fundamental operation for processing each element. The intuition is that arrays are indexed collections, and we often need to perform operations on each element or pair of elements. Using a for-loop with a range from 0 to n-1 (where n is the array length) allows us to access each index i and the element arr[i]. In pseudocode: for i in range(0, n): access arr[i]. This is O(n) time and O(1) space. This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to set up outer and inner loops for pair generation. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using range(0, n) vs range(0, n-1)), not handling empty arrays, and confusing indices with values. Real code pattern: arr = [2,3,4]; n = len(arr); for i in range(n): print(arr[i]).

examples.trace: n=3. Loop i from 0 to 2. i=0: arr[0]=5. i=1: arr[1]=10. i=2: arr[2]=15.
n=2. Loop i from 0 to 1. i=0: arr[0]=1. i=1: arr[1]=2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Definition
ladders.desc: Understand what GCD is: the largest positive integer that divides two numbers without a remainder.
ladders.explanation: GCD is the simplest prerequisite concept. The intuition is that for two integers a and b, their GCD is the biggest number that can exactly divide both a and b. For example, gcd(12,18)=6 because 6 divides both 12 and 18, and no larger number does. If gcd(a,b)=1, the numbers are coprime. GCD is always non-negative. To compute manually, list divisors: e.g., for 8 and 12, divisors of 8: 1,2,4,8; divisors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12; common divisors: 1,2,4; largest is 4, so gcd=4. This foundational concept feeds into L1 as the Euclidean algorithm computes GCD efficiently. Common mistakes include thinking GCD can be negative (it's defined as positive), confusing with LCM, or not handling zero (gcd(0,x)=|x|). Real code pattern: Concept only, no code here, but understanding is key for algorithm design.

examples.trace: Find divisors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12. Divisors of 18: 1,2,3,6,9,18. Common divisors: 1,2,3,6. Largest is 6, so gcd=6.
Divisors of 7: 1,7. Divisors of 5: 1,5. Common divisor: 1. Largest is 1, so gcd=1.
